title: Log File Parser - Final Touch

content.problemStatement:
Great! You have successfully built a **Log File Parser** using C++. 🎉

Your program can now **create, view, filter, search, and analyze logs** with ease through a simple menu-driven interface.

Here are some improvements you can try:


**Use a Database** – Replace the plain text file with a lightweight database like SQLite for structured storage and faster access.
**Enhanced Filtering** – Add support for combined filters (e.g., log level + keyword or date-based search).
**Log Export Feature** – Enable users to export filtered or complete logs to a CSV or JSON file for external use.
**Final Updates:**

Ensure all core functionalities (viewing, filtering, searching, statistics) work reliably.




Test edge cases such as:



Invalid or unsupported log levels
Empty or missing log files
Keywords that yield no results



Improve usability with clear messages, user prompts, and error handling.



Try implementing these improvements to make your C++ Log File Parser even more feature-rich and user-friendly! 🚀
constraints: LOG_LEVEL is one of DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: a line with integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000), followed by N lines each in the format "LOG_LEVEL KEYWORD: MESSAGE" where LOG_LEVEL ∈ {DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL}, KEYWORD contains no spaces, MESSAGE may contain spaces, and finally a line with the filter criteria – either "LOG_LEVEL KEYWORD" or just "LOG_LEVEL".
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output every log entry that satisfies the filter, preserving the original order, each on its own line in the same format as the input. If no entry matches, output a single line "No logs found.". Print a blank line between consecutive test cases.

sampleTestCases.input:
2
3
INFO start: System started
ERROR disk: Disk failure
WARNING memory: Low memory
ERROR disk
2
DEBUG init: Initialization complete
INFO load: Load successful
INFO load
sampleTestCases.output:
ERROR disk: Disk failure

INFO load: Load successful
sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: filter "ERROR disk" matches only the second log entry. Test case 2: filter "INFO load" matches the second log entry. A blank line separates the outputs of the two test cases.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
4
DEBUG config: Config loaded
DEBUG start: Starting service
INFO start: Service started
ERROR crash: Unexpected crash
DEBUG
sampleTestCases.output:
DEBUG config: Config loaded
DEBUG start: Starting service
sampleTestCases.explanation: Filter contains only the level "DEBUG", so both logs whose level is DEBUG are printed in their original order.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
2
WARNING temp: Temperature high
CRITICAL overload: System overload
INFO status
sampleTestCases.explanation: The filter "INFO status" looks for level INFO and keyword status, but neither log matches, so the fallback message is printed.

solution.approach: For each test case we store the N log lines. The filter line is split into level and optional keyword. While scanning the stored logs we extract the level and keyword from the part before the first colon. A log matches if its level equals the filter level and, when a keyword is provided, its keyword also equals the filter keyword. Matching logs are printed in the original order. If none match we print "No logs found.". A blank line separates outputs of different test cases. The algorithm is linear in the number of logs per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case to store the log strings.
